sentences.target_language: こんにちは、私の名前はショーンです。
sentences.romanized: konnichiwa, watashi no namae wa shoon desu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**私の名前は〜です** (watashi no namae wa ~ desu) = 'My name is ~': the standard self-introduction pattern. **私** (watashi, I/me) + **の** (no, possessive) + **名前** (namae, name) + **は** (wa, topic) + **〜です** (is).
**は** (wa) marks **名前** as the topic: 'As for my name, it is Sean'. Topic + **は** + comment is the fundamental Japanese sentence structure.
**こんにちは** (konnichiwa) = 'hello/good afternoon': the standard daytime greeting. Morning: **おはようございます** (ohayō gozaimasu); evening: **こんばんは** (konbanwa).

sentences.base_language: I have a lot of feelings.
sentences.target_language: 私はたくさんの感情があります。
sentences.romanized: watashi wa takusan no kanjou ga arimasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**たくさんの** (takusan no) = 'a lot of / many': **たくさん** (takusan, many/much) + **の** (possessive/modifying particle). It modifies the noun that follows: たくさんの感情 (many emotions).
**感情** (kanjō) = 'emotion(s) / feelings': **感** (kan, feeling/sense) + **情** (jō, emotion/feeling). A neutral, somewhat formal word. More casual alternatives: **気持ち** (kimochi, feeling/mood), **感じ** (kanji, sense/impression).
**感情があります** = 'I have emotions': **ある** (aru) is used for possession of abstract things like emotions, ideas, and talents.

sentences.base_language: When the New York Jets win I feel happy.
sentences.target_language: ニューヨーク・ジェッツが勝つと、私は嬉しいです。
sentences.romanized: nyuuyooku jettsu ga katsu to, watashi wa ureshii desu.
sentences.literal: New York Jets [subj] win if/when, I [topic] happy is.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜と (to) as a conditional particle expresses a natural or automatic consequence: '(whenever) X happens, Y results'. It's used for habitual or predictable reactions, not one-time decisions.

sentences.base_language: When the New York Jets lose I feel sad.
sentences.target_language: ニューヨーク・ジェッツが負けると、私は悲しいです。
sentences.romanized: nyuuyooku jettsu ga makeru to, watashi wa kanashii desu.
sentences.literal: New York Jets [subj] lose when, I [topic] sad is.
sentences.grammarNote:
**負ける** (makeru) = 'to lose (a game/competition)': the opposite of **勝つ** (katsu, to win). The subject takes **が**: チームが負ける (the team loses).
**悲しい** (kanashii) = 'sad': an い-adjective expressing sadness or sorrow. Deeper sadness: **辛い** (tsurai, painful/distressing). A more passing sadness: **寂しい** (sabishii, lonely/wistful).
The **〜と** conditional here means 'whenever ~ happens, this results' — expressing a predictable emotional reaction tied to an event.

sentences.base_language: Watching the new york jets can make me feel a lot of things.
sentences.target_language: ニューヨーク・ジェッツを見ると、いろいろな感情を感じます。
sentences.romanized: nyuuyooku jettsu wo miru to, iroirona kanjou wo kanjimasu.
sentences.literal: New York Jets [obj] watch when, various emotions [obj] feel.
sentences.grammarNote:
**いろいろな** (iroiro na) = 'various / all kinds of': **いろいろ** (iroiro, various) acts as a な-adjective before a noun. Similar words: **様々な** (samazama na, diverse/varied, more formal), **いくつかの** (ikutsuka no, several).
**感情を感じる** (kanjō wo kanjiru) = 'to feel emotions': note that **感** appears in both **感情** (emotion) and **感じる** (to feel). The **を** marks **感情** as the object of **感じる**.
**〜を見る** (wo miru) = 'to watch/look at ~': **見る** (miru, to see/watch) takes **を** for the thing watched — ジェッツを見る (watch the Jets).

**喜び** (yorokobi) = 'joy / delight': the noun form of **喜ぶ** (yorokobu, to rejoice/be pleased). **喜** (ki, joy) is the kanji for happiness and celebration — seen in **喜劇** (kigeki, comedy) and **喜寿** (kiju, 77th birthday celebration).
The verb **喜ぶ** = 'to feel joy / be delighted': プレゼントをもらって喜ぶ (be delighted to receive a gift).
Related words: **嬉しい** (ureshii, happy/glad, い-adjective) and **楽しい** (tanoshii, enjoyable/fun, い-adjective).

**怒り** (ikari) = 'anger / rage': the noun form of **怒る** (okoru/ikaru, to get angry). **怒** (do/nu, anger) appears in **怒鳴る** (donaru, to shout angrily) and **激怒** (gekido, furious rage).
**怒る** (okoru) in everyday speech means 'to get angry' or also 'to scold': 先生に怒られた (was scolded by the teacher — passive form).
Related: **腹が立つ** (hara ga tatsu, to get angry) — a more colloquial expression; literally 'the stomach stands up'.

**ストレス** (sutoresu) is a loanword from English 'stress', written in katakana as it is a foreign borrowing. Katakana is used for foreign loanwords, foreign names, and emphasis.
Common expressions: **ストレスがたまる** (sutoresu ga tamaru, stress builds up), **ストレスを発散する** (sutoresu wo hassan suru, relieve stress).
The native Japanese alternative for a similar concept is **疲れ** (tsukare, fatigue/exhaustion) or **プレッシャー** (puresshā, pressure, also a loanword).

**興奮** (kōfun) = 'excitement / agitation': **興** (kō, interest/excitement) + **奮** (fun, rouse/stir). The verb form is **興奮する** (kōfun suru, to get excited/agitated).
興奮している (kōfun shite iru) = 'I am excited' (ongoing state). Note that 興奮 can also imply being worked up or agitated, not just positive excitement.
A more purely positive synonym: **ワクワクする** (wakuwaku suru, to feel thrilled/excited, onomatopoeic) — very common in casual speech.

**不安** (fuan) = 'anxiety / unease / insecurity': **不** (fu, not/un-) + **安** (an, peace/ease). Literally 'un-peaceful'. The **不** prefix negates the noun/adjective it attaches to: 不便 (inconvenient), 不満 (dissatisfied), 不安 (uneasy).
**不安な** (fuan na) as a な-adjective: 不安な気持ち (an anxious feeling). **不安になる** (fuan ni naru) = 'to become anxious'.
Related: **心配** (shinpai, worry/concern) focuses on concern for a specific thing; **不安** is a more generalized feeling of unease or insecurity.

sentences.target_language: 今、私は穏やかな気持ちです
sentences.romanized: ima, watashi wa odayakana kimochi desu
sentences.grammarNote: 気持ち (kimochi) means 'feeling' or 'mood' as a noun. 穏やかな気持ちです literally says 'it is a calm feeling/mood', a common way to express an emotional state in Japanese.

sentences.base_language: Because it is not football season
sentences.target_language: なぜなら、今はアメフトのシーズンではないからです
sentences.romanized: nazenara, ima wa amefuto no shiizun dewa nai kara desu
sentences.literal: Because, now [topic] American-football [possessive] season is-not because is.
sentences.grammarNote: なぜなら〜からです (nazenara...kara desu) is a formal way to give a reason: 'the reason is that...'. なぜなら introduces the explanation, and からです closes it, together framing a complete causal statement.
